beats.text: For eight years the Transformer had no serious rival, and in 2026 that monopoly is finally being contested.

visual.sub: Two pressures squeeze attention from opposite ends

beats.text: Self-attention compares every token to every other token, so its compute grows quadratically with sequence length n.
State-space models replace softmax with an operator that costs only O(n) and carries a fixed-size recurrent state.
The second attack is extreme weight compression, pushing frozen models below the four-bit floor toward 1.58 effective bits.
Doubling the sequence length multiplies a quadratic attention layer's compute by four, while a linear SSM only doubles.
The honest caveat: the Transformer is contested, not dethroned, and the strongest shipping designs are hybrids.
A state-space model is a linear recurrence from control theory that folds the entire past into one fixed hidden state.

visual.sub: A constant-size summary of everything seen so far

beats.text: Each step updates the state from the input and reads an output off it, so decode memory stays constant.

visual.note: Linear recurrence; the state never grows

beats.text: Early SSMs lost to attention because a fixed recurrence treats every token identically and cannot choose what to remember.
Mamba makes the recurrence selective by letting B, C, and the step size be functions of the current input.

visual.note: A learnable gate: write, read, or forget per token

beats.text: Mamba-2's duality result trains in a quadratic matmul form and decodes in the linear recurrent form, getting both.
Mamba-2 matches a same-size Transformer on perplexity, but still loses on precise long-range recall from a lossy state.
Linear attention is the other route to O(n), and the duality result shows it is a close cousin of state-space models.

visual.sub: Drop the softmax, keep the linearity

beats.text: Replacing softmax with a feature map lets you reassociate the matmuls into a fixed-size running sum updated per token.

visual.note: Q(K^T V) is linear in n; no n-by-n matrix

beats.text: Dropping the softmax removes its sharp content-addressable lookup, so naive linear attention underperforms at recall.
The decisive insight is that you do not have to choose: mostly linear layers, with a thin interleaving of attention.
Jamba interleaves Mamba and Transformer blocks with mixture-of-experts, shipping a 256K-context production model.
The win is on cost and context length, not yet peak capability; no hybrid has displaced the best pure Transformers.
The second pressure is storage, and by 2026 the practical four-bit floor of Chapter 07 has moved lower.
A b-bit quantizer has 2^b levels, so each extra bit halves the rounding error but only adds an eighth of a byte.

visual.note: Error halves per bit; size grows by 1/8 byte

beats.text: Going from sixteen to eight to four bits is nearly free in quality; the pain starts below four where outliers dominate.
Three ideas stack to make it work: error-aware rounding, Hadamard rotation that spreads outliers, and fast unbiased quantizers.
BitNet b1.58 trains ternary weights from scratch, roughly 1.58 bits each, reframing the floor from four bits to less than two.
At two bits a 70-billion-parameter model occupies just 17.5 gigabytes, versus 140 at FP16, fitting one consumer card.
Step back and the 2026 frontier is a four-cornered race, not a coronation, with each contender trading a different axis.
The Transformer pays quadratic compute and a growing cache for exact recall and reasoning, but suffers on long context.
SSMs and linear attention buy linear compute and constant memory, winning throughput at the cost of precise retrieval.
Quantization is orthogonal, so the real 2026 deployment object is a hybrid in four-bit, not any single pure design.

visual.note: None of these are mutually exclusive

beats.text: SSMs have won the long-context efficiency argument; at million-token scale there is no contest with attention.
The likeliest outcome is not a successor but a blend, and knowing which operator to spend where is the new skill.
